title: The AI x Journalism Summit is two weeks away
content:
The AI x Journalism Summit is two weeks away

The second annual Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit lands May 13–14 in Baltimore, and we’re close to selling out. Claim your spot NOW!

Speakers include teams from The New York Times, CNN, ProPublica, Google, The Associated Press, NPR, The Boston Globe, The Philadelphia Inquirer, Minnesota Star Tribune, Mother Jones/Reveal, and Der Spiegel, across four tracks: Preview, Play, Adopt and Govern.

The schedule is live on the updated program page with sessions covering everything from secure local AI pipelines for investigations to build/buy/ignore decisions for newsroom leaders.

title: Every organization has a process, yours just isn't written down
content:
AI success stories tend to lead with the finale: the tool that saves hours, the workflow that runs itself, the demo that makes it all look inevitable. What you don't usually see is how anyone got there.

It can look like magic, or like something only a team with deep technical resources could pull off. But the solutions that actually move the needle for small organizations come from dedicated, methodical work: understanding what's broken, what's standing in the way. Defining these precisely, and finding the smallest first step that delivers real value. Solving that, then moving to the next most useful step.

That less visible work is where the real story is.

The second cohort of the Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab brought us three partners unlike any conventional newsroom: Trusting News, Prison Journalism Project, and Bellingcat. Each relies on a small team, between three and a f

creator: Paige Moody
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust

title: The Content Management System Is Dead. Long Live the Context Management System.
content:
The days I felt most alive as a journalist were the days I was out of the office, far from a desk doing actual reporting and documenting history.

When I was a reporter with The Associated Press at the start of my career, I'd grab my Reporter's Notebook and head to court hearings or press conferences, rush off to fires and earthquakes, or travel to cover diplomatic negotiations, elections or wars. Being a reporter gave me permission to speak to anyone so I could share their stories with the world. I'd then head back to my desk or wherever I was staying on assignment to write my article, distilling what I thought were the most important points into a few hundred words, and eventually entering my text into the main piece of software that writers use daily: the Content Management System, or CMS.

Most of the raw material I gathered went nowhere. My scrawlings in reporter's notebooks or typed notes, interview audio files, and dozens more photos than the one or two I u

title: Last day for $95 AI x Journalism Summit tickets, plus 12 new sessions
content:
One month until the AI x Journalism Summit! Today is last day for standard pricing

The second annual Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit lands in Baltimore on May 13-14. Tickets are still $95 through end of day today — they go up to $195 “Fashionably Late” pricing starting April 15. If you haven't yet made a decision, now is your moment.

The program also just got bigger. Twelve new sessions include ProPublica’s Ben Werdmuller and Dana Chiueh on secure local AI model pipelines for investigative work, Boston Globe’s Shira Center on build/buy/ignore decisions, Baltimore Banner’s Peter Rasmussen on News Detector, WBEZ’s Mark Chonofsky on reverse-engineering Whisper for archival audio, and two sessions from summit sponsor

content:

title: The best developers I've met this year aren't developers
content:
This is a guest post by Andrew Zigler, whose project was selected as the winner of the Hacks/Hackers AI hackathon with The Atlantic and Infactory in January 2026. We asked him to write about his work and approach to using AI as a developer.

At a hackathon hosted by The Atlantic, Infactory, and Hacks/Hackers, I spent most of my time doing something that probably looked strange from the outside: talking to my computer. Not coding, just talking and organizing my thinking out loud with voice-to-text, describing what I wanted to build and why. That process ended up winning first place.

The project was called The Beat, a platform for teachers to curate research from The Atlantic's archive. Students enter the collections and engage the sources, highlight

title: AI x Journalism Summit is six weeks away — travel stipends still available
content:
The Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Summit is May 13–14 in Baltimore — just six weeks away. If you’re working at the intersection of AI and journalism, this is the event to be at. Speakers from The New York Times, ProPublica, CNN, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Boston Globe, SPIEGEL and more will share how they’re actually building and governing AI in their newsrooms — from ProPublica’s “spreadsheet inference” approach to The New York Times’ work on designing AI tools that constrain hallucinations, plus hands-on workshops where you can build your own tools.

title: Newsroom AI Lab welcomes third cohort of partners
content:
The Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab is welcoming its third cohort of newsroom partners: Deep South Today, the San Fernando Valley Sun/El Sol and Tradeoffs.

The Lab pairs small and mid-sized newsrooms with technical advisors for three-month engagements. The goal is less about shipping a specific tool and more about helping teams figure out what to build and why. Each cohort starts by identifying real editorial problems, then scopes a realistic project and builds it collaboratively. Since launching in June 2025 with support from the Patrick J. McGovern Foundation, the program has worked with five organizations across two earlier cohorts.

"As journalism technologists, our role is to give reporters the tools and training they need to tell their best stories. Deep South Today's mission is to revitalize and sustain local journalism in the Deep South, and partnering with the Newsroom AI Lab gives us the opportunity to empower our journalists to explore and experiment with AI in ways that help
snippet: The Hacks/Hackers Newsroom AI Lab is welcoming its third cohort of newsroom partners: Deep South Today, the San Fernando Valley Sun/El Sol and Tradeoffs.The Lab pairs small and mid-sized newsrooms wit
creator: Burt Herman

title: AI advice from journalists who stopped talking and started building
content:
This article was originally published by The Poynter Institute. Poynter is Hacks/Hackers’ partner on AI ethics, governance and literacy, and is sponsoring the Govern track at our AI x Journalism Summit in Baltimore, May 13–14.

Of the 440 applications Texas Tribune chief product officer Darla Cameron received for an AI engineering role, 90 percent were junk.

Many appeared to be written using the very tools candidates were supposed to understand. “Here’s a short response that’ll work for this,” one application read — an obvious artifact of copying and pasting from ChatGPT.

The anecdote, shared during the Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Day at SXSW,
snippet: This article was originally published by The Poynter Institute. Poynter is Hacks/Hackers’ partner on AI ethics, governance and literacy, and is sponsoring the Govern track at our AI x Journalism Summi
creator: Alex Mahadevan

title: New sessions from ProPublica, CalMatters and more at the AI x Journalism Summit
content:
What happens when you embed an AI engineer in an investigative newsroom? At ProPublica, it led to “Spreadsheet Inference” — a method that combines the rigor of spreadsheet reporting with the scale of AI. Aaron Brezel will share how at the 2026 AI x Journalism Summit, May 13–14 in Baltimore.

He’s one of several new additions to the program. Derek Willis from the University of Maryland will show how to extract beat books from news archives using language models — and how to catch when they hallucinate sources. Sonya Quick from CalMatters built “Giving Editor,” an AI coaching system that sharpens editorial judgment without generating copy. Sean Vanderaa from Gigafact will detail how they use AI to index and analyze political figures’ public statements across platforms. And Peder Hammerskov from the Danish School of Media and Journalism will share frameworks for moving newsrooms from AI anxiety

title: Hacks/Hackers AI x Journalism Day in Austin (March 16)
content:
AI x Journalism House in Austin returns on Monday March 16, 2026. Join Hacks/Hackers for a full day of discussions, workshops, and networking at The Texas Tribune on Congress Avenue, in heart of SXSW festivities.

Registration

Schedule

9:45 am - 10:45 am | Hiring in the AI Era

AI tools now shape how candidates apply and how newsrooms hire. This session examines the risks — AI-generated applications, hiring fraud, and eroded signal — alongside the opportunities, drawing on the Texas Tribune's experience navigating these shifts as both a journalism organization and a tech-forward workplace.

Presenter:

title: Updated: 2026 AI x Journalism Summit program as of March 10
content:
The Philadelphia Inquirer is building an AI tool to track 90,000 local government meetings. The Minnesota Star Tribune launched audience-facing AI products at startup speed inside a 160-year-old newsroom. The New York Times is designing AI systems that work even when the models are confidently wrong.

They're all sharing how they did it at the 2026 AI x Journalism Summit, May 13–14 in Baltimore. We're still building out the full schedule — check out what's confirmed so far, and get in touch if you'd like to pitch a session.

Whether you want to prototype AI agents, stress-test documents with NotebookLM or design systems journalists trust, attendees will leave the AI x Journalism Summit with concrete skills, frameworks and new professional contacts. Four tracks — Preview, Play, Adopt and Govern — cover the full range from breakthrough experiments to responsible AI frameworks.

title: Updated: 2026 AI x Journalism Summit Program
content:
Hacks/Hackers and our host partners are convening the second AI x Journalism Summit in Baltimore from May 13–14. Schedule below — check back for late additions.

The summit brings together speakers from 52 news organizations, academic institutions and technology partners for 45 sessions across two days. The New York Times, The Associated Press, CNN, NPR, ProPublica, The Boston Globe, The Philadelphia Inquirer, The Baltimore Banner, CalMatters, WBEZ, the Center for Investigative Reporting, and PolitiFact are joining international peers from SPIEGEL, Ilta-Sanomat, and the Centre for Journalism Innovation and Development — alongside research from Harvard Kennedy School, Stanford, Columbia, CUNY, NYU, Northwestern, and Arizona State.

title: What we learned working with the Newsroom AI Lab’s first cohort
content:
Slow Down First. That’s How Newsrooms Move Faster With AI.

Most newsrooms assume they need an army of engineers or a big tech budget to experiment with AI. They don't. What they need is a repeatable process, the right collaborators and time to understand their problems before reaching for tools, AI or otherwise.

That's one of the takeaways from the first cohort of our Newsroom AI Lab. We partnered with Wisconsin Watch and The Connecticut Mirror to guide their teams through the process of beginning not with technology, but with conversations, interviewing colleagues across roles to surface the friction in – and opportunities to expand – their daily work.

From there, they each picked one problem to solve. We kept the scope tight and the timeline short. The goal wasn't to showcase AI, but to see what would actually help.
snippet: Slow Down First. That’s How Newsrooms Move Faster With AI.Most newsrooms assume they need an army of engineers or a big tech budget to experiment with AI. They don't. What they need is a repeatable pr
creator: Nevin Thompson
sourceDescription: Advancing media innovation to foster public trust
